comment_text.iti91:2.1: The unusual use of anta here is, I believe, a play on words. Per the commentary, it suggests the “least” of lifestyles, but it is also that which “makes an end” of suffering and future lives, as said just a few sentences below.
comment_text.iti91:2.4: The terms here follow the same sequence as in the Vinaya account of ordination. Generally, ordination should not be given in such cases, but if it is given, those performing it incur an offense of wrong-doing. | Soldiers joined the Sangha to escape military service, so the Buddha said one should not ordain those in service to a king (Kd 1:40.1.1). | Several rules regarding ordination of criminals were passed, the thrust of which is that wanted outlaws should not be ordained (Kd 1:41.1.1). | Another man ordained to escape debt (Kd 1:46.1.1). | Seventeen boys ordained as novices to get a nice livelihood (Kd 1:49.1.1). | The only item that does not straightforwardly correspond is “in fear” (bhayaṭṭa). In the Vinaya sequence, the ordination of slaves appears at this point (Kd 1:47.1.1). The connection is suggested by the verses of the water-carrier Puṇṇikā, who speaks of living “in fear” of her masters’ abuse and beatings.

root_text.iti91:2.3: Tañca kho etaṁ, bhikkhave, kulaputtā upenti atthavasikā, atthavasaṁ paṭicca;
root_text.iti91:2.4: neva rājābhinītā, na corābhinītā, na iṇaṭṭā, na bhayaṭṭā, na ājīvikāpakatā.
root_text.iti91:2.5: Api ca kho ‘otiṇṇamhā jātiyā jarāya maraṇena sokehi paridevehi dukkhehi domanassehi upāyāsehi dukkhotiṇṇā dukkhaparetā,
root_text.iti91:2.6: appeva nāma imassa kevalassa dukkhakkhandhassa antakiriyā paññāyethā’ti.
root_text.iti91:2.8: So ca hoti abhijjhālu kāmesu tibbasārāgo, byāpannacitto paduṭṭhamanasaṅkappo, muṭṭhassati asampajāno asamāhito vibbhantacitto pākatindriyo.
root_text.iti91:2.9: Seyyathāpi, bhikkhave, chavālātaṁ ubhatopadittaṁ majjhe gūthagataṁ neva gāme kaṭṭhatthaṁ pharati na araññe;
root_text.iti91:2.10: tathūpamāhaṁ, bhikkhave, imaṁ puggalaṁ vadāmi gihibhogā parihīno sāmaññatthañca na paripūretī”ti.
root_text.iti91:6.1: Ayampi attho vutto bhagavatā, iti me sutanti.

translation_text.iti91:1.1: This was said by the Buddha, the Perfected One: that is what I heard.
translation_text.iti91:2.1: “This is the last of lifestyles, mendicants, namely relying on alms.
translation_text.iti91:2.2: The world curses you: ‘You beggar, walking bowl in hand!’
translation_text.iti91:2.3: Yet earnest gentlemen take it up for a good reason.
translation_text.iti91:2.4: Not to escape a summons by a king or a summons for a bandit, or because you were in debt or in fear, or in order to make a living.
translation_text.iti91:2.5: Rather, because they think: ‘I’m swamped by rebirth, old age, and death; by sorrow, lamentation, pain, sadness, and distress. I’m swamped by suffering, mired in suffering.
translation_text.iti91:2.6: Hopefully I can make an end to this entire mass of suffering.’
translation_text.iti91:2.7: That’s how this gentleman has gone forth.
translation_text.iti91:2.8: Yet they covet sensual pleasures; they’re infatuated, full of ill will and malicious intent. They are unmindful, lacking situational awareness and immersion, with straying minds and undisciplined faculties.
translation_text.iti91:2.9: Suppose there was a firebrand for lighting a funeral pyre, burning at both ends, and smeared with dung in the middle. It couldn’t be used as timber either in the village or the wilderness.
translation_text.iti91:2.10: I say that individual is just like this. They’ve missed out on the pleasures of the lay life, and haven’t fulfilled the goal of the ascetic life.”
translation_text.iti91:3.1: “They’ve left behind the pleasures of the lay life,
translation_text.iti91:3.2: and miss out on the goal of the ascetic life.
translation_text.iti91:3.3: Ruining it, they throw it away,
translation_text.iti91:3.4: and perish like a funeral firebrand.
translation_text.iti91:4.1: Many who wear a scrap of ocher cloth
translation_text.iti91:4.3: Being wicked, they are reborn in hell
translation_text.iti91:5.1: It’d be better for the immoral and unrestrained
translation_text.iti91:5.4: than to eat the nation’s alms.”
translation_text.iti91:6.1: This too is a matter that was spoken by the Blessed One: that is what I heard.
